stem: A PTA is treating a client 6 weeks post-ACL reconstruction. Which exercise is most appropriate at this stage?
options: Closed kinetic chain mini-squats (0-60°) and stationary cycling
Open kinetic chain knee extension from 0-90°
rationale: 6 weeks post-ACL: closed kinetic chain exercises (mini-squats, leg press 0-60°) are safer as they minimize anterior tibial shear. Avoid full OKC extension early to protect the graft.

stem: Eccentric strengthening of the Achilles tendon is indicated for:
rationale: Alfredson's eccentric protocol is the gold standard for chronic Achilles tendinopathy: heavy-load eccentric heel drops, 3x15 reps, twice daily for 12 weeks.

stem: Progressive resistive exercises (PRE) follow which principle?
options: Overload principle: gradually increasing resistance as muscle adapts to the current load
Maintaining the same weight throughout rehab
rationale: Overload principle: muscles must be progressively challenged beyond current capacity to stimulate hypertrophy and strength gains. PRE systematically increases resistance.

stem: A client with rotator cuff tendinopathy. The PTA should begin strengthening in which position?
options: Below 90° of shoulder elevation to avoid impingement
rationale: Subacromial impingement worsens with shoulder elevation >90°. Initial strengthening below 90° avoids compressing inflamed tendons. Progress elevation as symptoms allow.

stem: Plyometric training for a post-ACL athlete begins with:
options: Double-leg activities (jumping/landing) before progressing to single-leg
No plyometrics are ever used post-ACL
rationale: Plyometric progression: bilateral → unilateral, low intensity → high, controlled → sport-specific. Usually begins at 4-6 months post-ACL reconstruction.

stem: Isometric exercises are appropriate when:
options: The joint should not move through range due to acute inflammation, post-surgical protection, or pain
The client has full ROM and no precautions
rationale: Isometrics maintain strength without joint movement. Appropriate for acute inflammation, joint effusion, post-surgical protection phases, and when joint motion causes pain.

stem: The PTA prescribes isotonic concentric-eccentric exercises for a client with quadriceps weakness. What is the correct description?
options: Concentric: muscle shortens under load; Eccentric: muscle lengthens under load
rationale: Concentric contraction: muscle shortens against resistance (lifting phase). Eccentric: muscle lengthens while controlling resistance (lowering phase). Eccentric produces more force capacity.

stem: Grade I and II joint mobilizations are used primarily for:
options: Pain management and maintaining joint play
Increasing range of motion at end range
rationale: Grade I (small amplitude, beginning of range) and II (large amplitude, mid-range): used for pain modulation through mechanoreceptor stimulation (gate theory). Not stretching joint capsule.

stem: Grade III and IV mobilizations are used for:
options: Increasing joint range of motion by stretching the joint capsule at end range
rationale: Grade III (large amplitude into resistance) and IV (small amplitude at end of range): address joint capsule restrictions and increase ROM. Require greater skill.

stem: The convex-concave rule states that:
options: When a convex surface moves on a stationary concave surface, the glide is opposite the direction of the bone movement
Glide always follows bone movement direction
rationale: Convex-on-concave: glide opposite to bone movement. Concave-on-convex: glide same direction. This determines mobilization direction for increasing specific ROM.

stem: Contraindications for joint mobilization include:
options: Active malignancy, fracture, joint instability, and acute inflammatory arthritis
rationale: Absolute contraindications: malignancy, fracture, infection, severe osteoporosis, joint instability, active RA flare, vascular disease. Relative: acute inflammation, pain.

stem: During normal gait, heel strike occurs at which phase?
rationale: Initial contact (heel strike) begins the stance phase of gait. The heel contacts the ground first in normal gait, initiating the loading response.

stem: Trendelenburg gait is caused by weakness of the:
options: Hip abductors (gluteus medius/minimus) on the stance leg
rationale: Trendelenburg: weak hip abductors on stance leg cause contralateral pelvic drop. The pelvis tilts down on the swing side. Compensatory lateral trunk lean may occur.

stem: A client demonstrates foot drop during swing phase. Which muscle is primarily affected?
rationale: Foot drop: inability to dorsiflex during swing phase, causing the foot to slap or drag. Commonly from peroneal nerve injury. May need AFO for clearance.

stem: Antalgic gait is characterized by:
options: Shortened stance phase on the painful limb to minimize weight-bearing time
rationale: Antalgic gait: decreased stance time on painful side, shortened step length, slower cadence. The body minimizes loading time to reduce pain.

stem: Step length versus stride length:
options: Step length is heel to heel of opposite feet; stride length is heel to heel of same foot (two steps)
Step length is longer than stride length
Stride length is measured during stance only
rationale: Step length: distance from one heel to the opposite heel. Stride length: two consecutive step lengths (same foot to same foot). Normal stride length ~1.4m.

stem: When using a standard walker, the proper gait pattern is:
options: Advance walker → step with involved leg → step with uninvolved leg
Step with uninvolved leg → advance walker → step with involved leg
Advance walker → step with uninvolved leg → step with involved leg
rationale: Walker gait sequence: advance walker first, then step with weaker/involved leg (using walker for support), then step with stronger/uninvolved leg.

stem: A client is non-weight-bearing (NWB) on the right leg. What assistive device and pattern is correct?
options: Axillary crutches with three-point NWB gait pattern
Standard cane on the right side
rationale: NWB requires crutches or walker with three-point gait: both crutches advance with the NWB leg, then the weight-bearing leg steps through. No weight on affected leg.

stem: When ascending stairs with a cane, the correct sequence is:
options: Up with the good leg first (good leg leads up)
Up with the involved leg first
Cane first, then both legs together
rationale: Stair training mnemonic: 'Up with the good, down with the bad.' Ascending: uninvolved leg first → cane/involved leg follows. Uses stronger leg to lift body weight.

stem: A Lofstrand (forearm) crutch differs from an axillary crutch in that:
options: It has a forearm cuff allowing the hand to be freed without dropping the crutch
It provides more stability than an axillary crutch
It cannot be used for NWB patients
It requires less upper body strength
rationale: Lofstrand crutches: forearm cuff allows hand release for doorknobs etc. Requires more skill and upper body strength than axillary crutches. Common for long-term use.

stem: A single-point cane is held in which hand?
options: The hand opposite the affected lower extremity
The hand on the same side as the affected leg
Either hand regardless of affected side
rationale: Cane is held contralateral to the affected leg. This mimics normal gait mechanics where the opposite arm swings forward with each step, reducing lateral trunk lean.

stem: Balance training progression should follow:
options: Stable surface → unstable surface, bilateral → unilateral stance, eyes open → eyes closed
Start on the most unstable surface
Skip progression and go to sport-specific tasks
rationale: Balance progression: stable/firm → foam/wobble board, wide BOS → narrow BOS, bilateral → single leg, eyes open → closed, static → dynamic, no perturbation → perturbation.

stem: The Berg Balance Scale is used to:
options: Assess functional balance in older adults through 14 tasks scored 0-4, identifying fall risk
rationale: Berg Balance Scale: 14 items (sit-to-stand, standing, transfers, reaching, turning, etc.), scored 0-4 each. Total <45/56 indicates fall risk. Widely used in geriatrics.

stem: A client with vestibular hypofunction. The PTA should include:
options: Gaze stabilization exercises (VOR adaptation) and habituation exercises for motion sensitivity
rationale: Vestibular rehabilitation: VOR (vestibulo-ocular reflex) adaptation exercises (focus on target during head movement), habituation (repeated exposure to provoking movements), balance training.

stem: A PTA treating a client 3 days post-ischemic stroke. Early mobilization goals include:
options: Sitting at edge of bed, standing with assist, and early supported ambulation as tolerated
rationale: Early mobilization (24-48 hours post-stroke): progressive sitting, standing, and walking as tolerated. Evidence supports early mobilization for improved functional outcomes, but avoid excessive early activity.

stem: Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) diagonal patterns involve:
options: Multiplanar movements combining flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and rotation
rationale: PNF diagonals: D1 flexion (flexion/adduction/ER) and D1 extension (extension/abduction/IR), D2 flexion (flexion/abduction/ER) and D2 extension (extension/adduction/IR). Combine 3 planes.

stem: Neurodevelopmental treatment (NDT/Bobath) focuses on:
options: Facilitating normal movement patterns while inhibiting abnormal tone and reflexes
rationale: NDT: handling techniques to inhibit abnormal tone, facilitate normal postural reactions, and promote quality movement patterns. Emphasizes trunk control and weight-shifting.

stem: Body weight-supported treadmill training (BWSTT) for stroke patients:
options: Provides external support to allow task-specific gait practice at reduced body weight
Increases fall risk compared to overground walking
rationale: BWSTT: harness system supports 20-40% body weight, allowing early task-specific gait training. Evidence supports improved gait speed and endurance post-stroke.

stem: Functional electrical stimulation (FES) for foot drop in stroke:
options: Stimulates the peroneal nerve/tibialis anterior during swing phase to assist dorsiflexion
rationale: FES activates the common peroneal nerve/tibialis anterior during swing phase, providing ankle dorsiflexion for foot clearance. Can be used therapeutically or as a neuroprosthesis.

stem: A client with T10 paraplegia. Expected functional outcomes include:
options: Independent transfers, ambulation with KAFOs and crutches for exercise, wheelchair for primary mobility
rationale: T10 paraplegia: full UE function, hip flexors/extensors available, intact abdominals. Independent in wheelchair, transfers, may ambulate with KAFOs for exercise distance.

stem: Heterotopic ossification (HO) in SCI is characterized by:
options: Formation of abnormal bone in periarticular soft tissues below the level of injury
Increased bone density in the spine
rationale: HO: abnormal bone formation in periarticular soft tissues below the neurological level. Signs: decreased ROM, swelling, warmth. Most common in hips and knees. Limits functional mobility.

stem: Orthostatic hypotension in new SCI patients is managed by:
options: Progressive tilt table training, abdominal binder, compression stockings, and gradual position changes
Keeping the patient supine at all times
Rapid position changes to train blood pressure
rationale: SCI disrupts sympathetic vasomotor control. Progressive tilt table training, compressive garments, abdominal binder, and gradual position changes help cardiovascular adaptation.

stem: Therapeutic ultrasound at 1 MHz is used for:
options: Deep tissue heating at depths of 3-5 cm
rationale: 1 MHz: deeper penetration (3-5 cm), used for deep structures (hip joint, deep muscles). 3 MHz: superficial structures (1-2 cm depth, tendons, superficial muscles).

stem: Continuous ultrasound produces primarily:
rationale: Continuous US: 100% duty cycle, produces thermal effects (increased blood flow, tissue extensibility, metabolism). Pulsed US: lower duty cycle, primarily nonthermal/mechanical effects.

stem: Contraindications for therapeutic ultrasound include:
options: Over areas of malignancy, active growth plates in children, over the eyes, and over the pregnant uterus
rationale: US contraindications: malignancy, epiphyseal plates in children, eyes, reproductive organs, pregnant uterus, pacemakers, thrombophlebitis, metal implants (relative), acute inflammation.

stem: The appropriate ultrasound intensity for thermal effects is:
rationale: Thermal US: 1.0-2.0 W/cm² continuous for tissue heating. Nonthermal: 0.5-1.0 W/cm² pulsed (20% duty cycle). Higher intensities risk tissue damage.

stem: TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) works primarily through:
options: Gate control theory: stimulating large-diameter A-beta fibers to block pain transmission at the spinal cord level
rationale: Conventional TENS: high frequency (80-150 Hz), low intensity stimulates A-beta mechanoreceptors that 'close the gate' to pain signals at the dorsal horn. Also endorphin release.

stem: NMES (neuromuscular electrical stimulation) parameters for strengthening include:
options: Frequency 30-50 Hz, on/off ratio 1:3 to 1:5, intensity to produce visible contraction
rationale: NMES strengthening: 30-50 Hz for fused tetanic contraction, on/off ratio allows recovery (1:3 to 1:5), intensity sufficient for visible contraction, 10-20 contractions per session.

stem: Iontophoresis uses direct current to:
options: Drive charged medication molecules through the skin into underlying tissues
rationale: Iontophoresis: uses direct current to drive ionized medications (dexamethasone, lidocaine, acetic acid) transdermally. Like-charges repel: positive drug on positive electrode.

stem: Contraindications for electrical stimulation include:
options: Over a demand-type pacemaker, over the carotid sinus, and over the anterior cervical region
rationale: ES contraindications: demand pacemaker (may interfere), carotid sinus (may cause bradycardia/syncope), transthoracic application, over malignancy, over gravid uterus.

stem: Cryotherapy is applied to an acute injury primarily to:
options: Reduce inflammation, edema, pain, and metabolic demands of injured tissue
Increase blood flow to the area
rationale: Cryotherapy in acute injury: vasoconstriction reduces edema/bleeding, decreased metabolism reduces secondary hypoxic injury, decreased nerve conduction velocity reduces pain.

stem: Moist hot packs should be applied for:
options: 15-20 minutes with 6-8 layers of toweling between the pack and skin
rationale: Moist heat: 15-20 minutes, 6-8 layers of toweling to prevent burns. Check skin at 5 minutes. Contraindicated over insensate skin, acute inflammation, malignancy.

stem: Contrast bath therapy alternates between:
options: Warm water (100-110°F) and cold water (55-65°F) to promote vascular pumping and reduce edema
Two different temperatures of hot water
rationale: Contrast bath: warm (3-4 min) → cold (1 min), 3-5 cycles. Alternating vasodilation/vasoconstriction creates 'vascular pumping' to reduce edema. Begin and end with warm.

stem: Phase I cardiac rehab begins:
options: During the acute hospital stay, typically 24-48 hours after cardiac event or surgery
rationale: Phase I: inpatient, begins 24-48 hours post-event. Focus: early mobilization, patient education, ADL training, vitals monitoring, activity progression per MET levels.

stem: During exercise, the PTA should stop the activity if the patient demonstrates:
options: Systolic BP drop >10 mmHg with increasing workload, chest pain, or new arrhythmia
Normal fatigue at end of session
rationale: Stop exercise: SBP drop >10 mmHg, chest pain/angina, new arrhythmia, excessive fatigue/dyspnea, dizziness, pallor/cyanosis, HR exceeding target or failing to rise with exercise.

stem: Rate of perceived exertion (RPE) on the Borg 6-20 scale for moderate exercise is:
rationale: Moderate exercise intensity: RPE 12-14 ('somewhat hard'). Borg scale: 6 (rest) to 20 (maximal). RPE correlates with %HRmax and VO2max. Talk test: can talk but not sing.

stem: The 6-minute walk test (6MWT) measures:
options: Functional exercise capacity and cardiopulmonary endurance
rationale: 6MWT: submaximal functional test measuring distance walked in 6 minutes. Reflects functional capacity for ADLs. Normal: >400m. Used in cardiac, pulmonary, and neurological rehab.

stem: Pursed-lip breathing is taught to COPD patients because:
options: It creates back pressure to prevent airway collapse and improve CO2 elimination
rationale: Pursed-lip breathing: creates positive pressure during exhalation, prevents premature airway closure (especially in emphysema), promotes complete exhalation, and improves gas exchange.

stem: Diaphragmatic breathing instruction involves:
options: Client places hand on abdomen, inhales slowly through nose allowing abdomen to rise, exhales through pursed lips
rationale: Diaphragmatic breathing: enhances diaphragm function, reduces accessory muscle use, improves ventilation efficiency. Abdominal rise on inhalation confirms diaphragm engagement.

stem: Incentive spirometry post-surgery is used to:
options: Prevent atelectasis by encouraging sustained maximal inspiration
rationale: Incentive spirometry: visual feedback device encouraging slow, sustained maximal inspiration. Prevents post-surgical atelectasis by expanding collapsed alveoli. Goal: 10 breaths per hour.

stem: Proper body mechanics for a PTA assisting with transfers includes:
options: Wide base of support, close to the patient, back straight, lift with legs, avoid twisting
Narrow stance, reaching away from body
Bending at the waist to reach the patient
rationale: Body mechanics: wide BOS, center of gravity low, close to load, lift with legs (not back), engage core, avoid twisting, pivot feet instead. Prevents PTA injury.

stem: A mechanical lift (Hoyer) is indicated when:
options: The client requires maximum assist of two or is unable to bear any weight and is too heavy for manual transfer
The client requires minimal assist only
rationale: Mechanical lifts: used when clients cannot weight-bear and are too heavy/dependent for safe manual transfers. Reduces staff injury risk. Required when patient requires max assist x2.

stem: When guarding a client during ambulation, the PTA should position:
options: Slightly behind and to the side of the weaker/involved extremity
At a distance of 10 feet behind
rationale: Guarding: slightly behind and to the weaker side with hands ready at gait belt and shoulder. This position allows the PTA to support the client if they lose balance toward the weaker side.

stem: Risk factors for falls in older adults include:
options: Polypharmacy, visual impairment, lower extremity weakness, environmental hazards, and history of falls
rationale: Fall risk factors: age >65, fall history, medications (sedatives, antihypertensives), visual impairment, LE weakness, balance deficits, cognitive impairment, environmental hazards.

stem: The Timed Up and Go (TUG) test identifies fall risk when:
options: The client takes longer than 12-14 seconds to complete the test
The client finishes in under 5 seconds
The client stands up without difficulty
rationale: TUG: stand from chair, walk 3 meters, turn, walk back, sit. >12-14 seconds suggests increased fall risk. Simple, reliable, widely used in clinical and community settings.

stem: A home safety evaluation should assess:
options: Lighting, throw rugs, grab bars, stair railings, clutter, and footwear appropriateness
rationale: Home safety: adequate lighting (nightlights), remove throw rugs, install grab bars (bathroom), secure railings, reduce clutter, non-skid footwear, raised toilet seat, shower bench.

stem: A typically developing infant achieves independent sitting by approximately:
rationale: Independent sitting: 6-8 months. Progression: head control (3-4 mo), supported sitting (5-6 mo), independent sitting (6-8 mo), pulling to stand (9-10 mo).

stem: Cruising (walking while holding onto furniture) typically occurs at:
rationale: Cruising: 9-12 months. Follows pulling to stand. Precursor to independent walking. Develops lateral weight shifting and single-leg stance stability.

stem: The Moro reflex should integrate (disappear) by:
rationale: Moro reflex: present at birth, integrates by 4-6 months. Persistence beyond 6 months may indicate neurological abnormality. Involves arm abduction, extension, then adduction with startle.

stem: Asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR) integration occurs by:
rationale: ATNR: head turns → arm/leg extend on face side, flex on skull side ('fencing posture'). Present from birth, integrates by 4-6 months. Persistence interferes with rolling, reaching, hand-to-mouth.

stem: A child with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy primarily demonstrates:
options: Bilateral lower extremity involvement with increased tone, scissoring gait, and toe walking
rationale: Spastic diplegia: bilateral LE involvement predominant, may have mild UE involvement. Characterized by scissoring (hip adduction), toe walking, crouched gait pattern.

stem: Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS) Level III means:
options: Walks with assistive device indoors and outdoors, may use wheelchair for long distances
rationale: GMFCS Level I: walks without limitations. Level II: walks with limitations. Level III: walks with assistive device. Level IV: self-mobility limited, may use powered wheelchair. Level V: transported.

stem: Serial casting for a child with equinus contracture in CP:
options: Applies progressive stretch through sequential casts changed every 1-2 weeks to increase dorsiflexion
Is applied once and removed after 6 months
Uses casts in the opposite direction
rationale: Serial casting: progressive stretching through sequential casts, each applied after achieving maximum gentle stretch. Changed every 1-2 weeks, typically 4-6 casts. Followed by AFO/splinting.

stem: The 'O' (Objective) section of a SOAP note should include:
options: Measurable data: ROM values, strength grades, gait distance, vital signs, and specific test results
rationale: Objective data: quantifiable observations and measurements — ROM (degrees), MMT (grades), distances walked, time measurements, vital signs, functional test scores, specific observations.

stem: When should the PTA communicate with the supervising PT?
options: When there is a significant change in patient status, new findings, or when treatment goals need modification
Only at the end of the episode of care
Only when asked by the PT
rationale: PTA-PT communication: significant status changes, adverse reactions, goal modifications needed, new diagnoses, patient/family concerns, discharge readiness, and any clinical uncertainties.

stem: Functional outcome measures commonly used in PT include:
options: FIM (Functional Independence Measure), TUG, Berg Balance Scale, and 6MWT
rationale: Functional outcomes: FIM (independence in ADLs), TUG (mobility/fall risk), Berg (balance), 6MWT (endurance), DASH (upper extremity), ODI (low back), and condition-specific measures.

stem: The APTA Code of Ethics principle of justice means:
options: Providing fair and equitable treatment to all patients regardless of background
rationale: Justice: fair and equitable access to physical therapy services. PTs/PTAs should not discriminate based on race, gender, age, disability, socioeconomic status, or other characteristics.

stem: A PTA observes another PTA performing a technique outside the scope of PTA practice. The appropriate action is:
options: Report the observation to the supervising PT and/or facility supervisor
Ignore it since it is not their responsibility
Confront the PTA publicly in front of the patient
rationale: Scope of practice concerns should be reported through proper channels: supervising PT, facility supervisor, and if necessary, the state licensing board. Patient safety is paramount.

stem: Static stretching should be held for a minimum of:
options: 30 seconds for optimal lengthening of muscle fibers
rationale: Research supports 30-second static stretches for optimal elongation. 15-30 seconds minimum. In elderly populations, 60 seconds may provide additional benefit. 2-4 repetitions recommended.

stem: PNF stretching techniques include:
options: Contract-relax and hold-relax methods that use autogenic or reciprocal inhibition
rationale: PNF stretching: contract-relax (isometric contraction → relaxation → passive stretch). Hold-relax (isometric → relax → stretch). Uses autogenic inhibition (GTO) or reciprocal inhibition for greater ROM gains.

stem: Contraindications for aggressive stretching include:
options: Recent fracture, acute inflammation, joint hypermobility, and compromised soft tissue integrity
rationale: Stretching contraindications: unstable fracture, acute inflammation/infection, joint hypermobility/instability, hematoma, shortened tissue providing functional stability, acute muscle tears.

stem: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) affecting the posterior semicircular canal is treated with:
rationale: Epley maneuver: repositions otoconia (crystals) from the posterior SCC back to the utricle. Highly effective (80-95% success rate). Specific head positions maintained for 30-60 seconds each.

stem: The Dix-Hallpike test is used to diagnose:
options: BPPV involving the posterior semicircular canal
rationale: Dix-Hallpike: positive result (nystagmus + vertigo with latency and fatigue) indicates posterior canal BPPV. Head is turned 45° then rapidly brought to supine with head hanging.

stem: Habituation exercises for vestibular dysfunction involve:
options: Repeated exposure to movements that provoke mild dizziness until the brain adapts and symptoms diminish
Using medication to suppress symptoms during exercise
rationale: Habituation: systematic repeated exposure to symptom-provoking positions/movements. The vestibular system adapts through neural plasticity, reducing symptom intensity over time.

stem: Forward head posture is commonly associated with:
options: Tight suboccipitals and pectorals, weak deep cervical flexors and lower trapezius
rationale: Forward head posture: tight suboccipitals, SCM, upper trapezius, pectorals. Weak: deep cervical flexors, lower trapezius, serratus anterior. Treatment: stretch tight, strengthen weak.

stem: Upper crossed syndrome includes:
options: Tight upper trapezius/levator scapulae and pectorals, weak deep cervical flexors and lower trapezius/serratus anterior
Tight abdominals and weak back extensors
rationale: Upper crossed syndrome (Janda): 'X' pattern of tight (upper trap/levator, pecs) crossed with weak (deep cervical flexors, lower trap/serratus anterior). Leads to forward head and rounded shoulders.
